page.content: The data presented in Table 1 provides a comprehensive overview of the effects of ACE inhibitors (ACEi) and angiotensin receptor blockers (ARB) on the AdiC transporter in *Escherichia coli*. This analysis is essential in understanding the broader implications of these pharmacological agents on various biological systems. Notably, the table includes metrics such as the number of studies that reported a “Reduced” effect, which accounted for 12% of the total observations, and the “Increased” effects, which constituted a significant 85%.

lines.text: The data presented in Table 1 provides a comprehensive overview of the effects of ACE inhibitors (ACEi) and angiotensin
receptor blockers (ARB) on the AdiC transporter in *Escherichia coli*. This analysis is essential in understanding the broader
implications of these pharmacological agents on various biological systems. Notably, the table includes metrics such as the
number of studies that reported a “Reduced” effect, which accounted for 12% of the total observations, and the “Increased”

page.content: The predominant outcome indicated that the majority of studies (44 out of 52) observed no change in the activity of the transporter when exposed to these agents. Additionally, the resolution of the structural data, specified in angstroms (Å), provides critical insight into the conformational dynamics of the protein under study. The inclusion of the PDB ID and protomeric composition further enriches the understanding of the molecular context in which these effects are observed. These findings underscore the need for continued research into the interactions between ACEi/ARB and transport proteins, as they may have significant implications for therapeutic strategies targeting cardiovascular and renal disorders.
